metadata.file_name: 31. Zhang & Cutumisu. (2024). Predicting the Mathematics Literacy of Resilient Student.pdf
metadata.title: Predicting the Mathematics Literacy of Resilient Students from High‐performing Economies: A Machine Learning Approach

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study uses PISA 2022 microdata and restricts the analytic sample to resilient students defined by OECD criteria (bottom quarter of ESCS within economy and top quarter of mathematics literacy within economy), then further splits/combines them into Eastern vs Western culture groups. The authors explicitly incorporate final student weights (W_FSTUWT) into the ML model development and validation to adjust for PISA’s complex survey design features (stratification, clustering, and unequal selection probabilities), aiming for representativeness of the target student population. Missing values are imputed prior to modeling using missForest (RF-based imputation) in R, and the resulting datasets are used within nested cross-validation. The manuscript does not report replicate-weight methods (e.g., BRR/jackknife), so uncertainty estimation via replicate weights is not described.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Resilient students were selected following OECD PISA 2022 guidelines: (1) within each economy, students in the bottom quarter of ESCS were identified; (2) within that ESCS-disadvantaged group, students whose mathematics literacy was in the top quarter within the same economy were retained. To determine the top quarter of mathematics literacy, the authors computed an average of 10 mathematics plausible values (PV) per student, ranked students by this PV average, and selected the top 25% (while also meeting the bottom-ESCS criterion). The final resilient-student sample was then split into Eastern vs Western culture datasets based on Triandis (1995) and analyzed with nested cross-validation; Western cultures combined Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Czech Republic, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, Ireland, Latvia, Netherlands, New Zealand, Poland, Slovenia, Sweden, Switzerland, and the UK, while Eastern cultures combined Chinese Taipei, Hong Kong, Japan, Korea, Macao, and Singapore.

main_findings.dataset_used: PISA 2022 Mathematics literacy (resilient students; Eastern cultures)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Random Forest (Eastern): RMSE (Average across PVs): 44.47 (Validation/Test details by PV reported in Table 5); R² (Average across PVs): 0.01; MAE (Average across PVs): 35.40. Lasso (Eastern): RMSE (Average across PVs): 45.96; R² (Average across PVs): 0.00; MAE (Average across PVs): 36.44. Model comparison across cultures via Kruskal-Wallis/Dunn indicates both RF and LR perform better in Western than Eastern cultures (lower RMSE/MAE, higher R²).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2022 Mathematics literacy data for resilient students in Eastern cultures, the study leveraged mathematics self-efficacy (21st-century and formal/applied), mathematics anxiety, effort/persistence, and working-in-household indicators to predict mathematics literacy (PVs 1–10) with Random Forest and Lasso; Random Forest achieved lower RMSE/MAE than Lasso and the most important predictors in Eastern cultures included MATHEF21, MATHEFF, and ANXMAT. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.dataset_used: PISA 2022 Mathematics literacy (resilient students; Western cultures)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Random Forest (Western): RMSE (Average across PVs): 42.27; R² (Average across PVs): 0.06; MAE (Average across PVs): 32.65. Lasso (Western): RMSE (Average across PVs): 40.25; R² (Average across PVs): 0.11; MAE (Average across PVs): 31.76. Across cultures, both RF and LR show better performance in Western than Eastern cultures (lower RMSE/MAE and higher R²).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2022 Mathematics literacy data for resilient students in Western cultures, the study leveraged mathematics self-efficacy (formal/applied), family support, mathematics anxiety, home possessions, and cognitive activation to predict mathematics literacy (PVs 1–10) with Random Forest and Lasso; the most important feature in Western cultures was MATHEFF, and overall predictive performance was stronger in Western than Eastern cultures. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: The study analyzed PISA 2022 resilient students (N=4654) across 23 economies, defining resilience as bottom-quarter ESCS and top-quarter mathematics literacy within each economy. Mathematics literacy was modeled using the 10 mathematics plausible values (PVs 1–10) as response variables, with Random Forest and Lasso trained using nested 5-fold cross-validation and incorporating final student weights (W_FSTUWT). Missing questionnaire data were imputed using missForest (RF-based imputation). Predictive performance was consistently better for Western cultures than Eastern cultures: both models showed lower RMSE and MAE and higher R² in Western contexts. Feature importance differed by culture: Eastern cultures emphasized mathematics self-efficacy (21st-century) and mathematics anxiety, whereas Western cultures highlighted mathematics self-efficacy (formal/applied) as the predominant predictor. The paper focuses on prediction rather than causal pathways, and feature importance should not be interpreted as causal effects.
